items.title: North America: Road Ahead Second-Half 2026
items.summary: We remain constructive on the full-year U.S. equity outlook, and expect the broadening theme to assert itself in the second half. While AI tailwinds will remain critical to the U.S. equity narrative, we see opportunity in stock selection across industries, with more detail in this note.

items.title: U.S. Aerospace & Defense: "Aerospace First, Defense Next"
items.summary: Our second-quarter tactical framework has been "Aerospace First, Defense Next" given our belief that aerospace stocks would lead the group out of its post-conflict pullback. We now see risk/reward as increasingly asymmetric across defense stocks as well. We offer further details herein.

items.title: Global Gas and Power Insights: American Exceptionalism!
items.summary: Strong U.S. LNG exports should continue to loosen up LNG and European gas supply-demand balances over the next 12 to 18 months. Meanwhile, the U.S. gas balance would be marginally tighter, though still overwhelmed by strong production expected from the Permian and Haynesville.

items.title: The Speculative Grade Beat: Why We're Cutting Duration
items.summary: Base rates — not spreads — account for the lion's share of high-yield total yield, a dynamic whose effects we explore in this note. Having flipped our Asset Allocation preference to loans, we now shorten duration in our High Yield Factor Framework by moving to underweight Long Duration.

items.title: U.S. High Grade: Fundamentals Wrap-Up
items.summary: Our first-quarter review of investment-grade issuers’ credit fundamental trends shows a capex cycle that keeps accelerating, with the trailing 12-month capex share of EBITDA reaching 49.2%, while the broader market’s balance sheet stays remarkably intact. We offer further review herein.

items.title: Japan FX: Nobody's Stopping the Yen's Depreciation
items.summary: The rise in Japanese interest rates is tapering off, which should support gains by the JPY over the longer term. But it wouldn't be surprising if it also dampened the motivation of the Takaichi government and U.S. authorities to minimize the decline in the JPY, as we explain in this note.

items.title: Latin America Economic Outlook & Strategy: A Shift Right and Reform
items.summary: Latin America's political shift to the right improves the outlook for reform but doesn't guarantee it, as anti-incumbent sentiment has created fragmented governments that lack the legislative support to pass crucial and often unpopular fiscal reforms. We offer further analysis herein.

items.title: U.S. Banks: Second-Quarter Earnings Preview
items.summary: As part of our earnings preview, we delve into a key recent debate: “What's more important, net interest margin or net interest income?” We also unveil a trio of rating changes: upgrading TCBI (Neutral from Sell) while downgrading BKU (Sell from Neutral) and TFC (Neutral from Buy).

items.title: U.S. Hiring Remains Soft Despite Stronger Payrolls
items.summary: Job openings remained elevated in the May JOLTS report but have been quite volatile and not necessarily a reliable sign of demand for workers. Indeed.com job postings suggest a pullback in openings ahead. More important in May's JOLTS report? Signs that labor-market churn remains very low.

items.title: We're Raising Our Forecasts for South Korea's GDP
items.summary: We're raising our forecast for South Korea's 2026 GDP by 0.4 ppt to 3.5% given surprisingly resilient April-May hard data and a potential second fiscal stimulus. We also raise our 2027 and 2028 GDP forecasts 0.2 ppt each to 3.0% and 2.5%, respectively. We offer further analysis herein.

items.title: North America Trucking: Private Trucker Panel Takeaways
items.summary: We hosted a discussion about the state of the trucking industry with executives from private carriers Cheema Freightlines and Paper Transport, and found confidence in improving conditions given “permanent and structural changes in capacity.” We detail our key takeaways in this note.

items.title: Must C: Overcoming Gridlock 2.0
items.summary: Power-grid expansion appears unable to keep pace with strong demand and supply growth, constrained by supply-chain challenges and many regions' permitting frameworks. In this report we explore solutions to ease grid constraints and accelerate AI deployment and energy-security transitions.

items.title: Mexico Faces Lack of a Short-Term Catalyst, Eyes USMCA Updates
items.summary: USMCA talks have missed the deadline and may extend into 2027, suppressing investment. We reiterate our 2026 year-end S&P IPC target price of 70,000 and set a mid-2027 target of 73,000, with the S&P IPC likely range-bound in the second half. We offer notes on other asset classes herein.

items.title: Global Emerging Markets Strategy: The EM Strategist
items.summary: We believe high-yielding emerging markets can stay resilient despite rising U.S. real yields and a stronger USD. Many EM central banks have maintained less dovish policies than anticipated, supporting high real rates, building substantial FX reserves and creating selective opportunities.

items.title: U.S. Semiconductors: DRAM Supply Is the New Ranking Metric
items.summary: Following earnings, we update our AI compute semis rankings. With DRAM memory shortages the biggest constrain on compute supply, our updated rankings assign the highest weight to memory supply allocation. NVDA is our top mega-cap, followed by AVGO; AMD tops large caps, with CBRS second.

items.title: U.S. Economics Weekly: The Economy Is Not Overheating
items.summary: Front-end Treasury yields have fallen from their post-FOMC highs. Upcoming data present a trend toward softer consumer spending, declining real incomes and rising continuing jobless claims, all sharply at odds with the narrative that the U.S. economy is at risk of “overheating.”

items.title: Global Quantitative Insights: Factors, Flows & Fault Lines
items.summary: Price Momentum continues to anchor global style outperformance, proving the most resilient factor year-to-date. Shorter-term reversals, however, suggest a transitionary phase for style leadership may be approaching, a shift we explore in detail in this note.

items.title: North America Internet: Digital Postcard from Cannes
items.summary: We left Cannes incrementally positive on the broader online advertising ecosystem, the benefits of AI and agents, and the growing importance of data, commerce, and brand visibility as the web evolves from queries to quests. We offer key takeaways and review our top picks.

items.title: We're Upgrading Murata to Buy
items.summary: We upgrade Murata to Buy and raise our target price, reflecting revisions to earnings forecasts, changes to valuation assumptions and a shift in our base year. We don't anticipate company-led price revisions for general-purpose products, but such revisions would create additional upside.

items.title: We Initiate Coverage of INNIO Group at Neutral
items.summary: We think INNIO’s well-established position as a public “pure play” reciprocating-engine manufacturer supports a significant multi-year earnings growth opportunity, but see the near-term risk/reward as relatively balanced. We initiate coverage with a Neutral rating and a $42 target price.

items.title: Africa Economics & Strategy Weekly: Questions About Tanzania
items.summary: After meetings with government and the private sector, we examine whether new economic trends are restoring a more seasonal pattern to the Tanzanian shilling following a period marked by considerable volatility. We also consider several unresolved questions about Tanzania's macro outlook.

items.title: U.S. Rates Weekly: Front-End Flatteners and Long-End Value
items.summary: We lean into our front-end flattener paired with a received July FOMC position ahead of the jobs report. Further out the curve, we remain constructive on duration, with our 10y view anchored by our expectation for core CPI to fall into year-end. We offer further strategic thoughts herein.

items.title: Emerging Markets Economic Outlook & Strategy: The Next Shock
items.summary: A Super El Niño could mean a significant inflationary shock for emerging markets, complicating the macro outlook even as lower energy prices offer relief. The primary risk stems from food inflation, disproportionately impacting those with high food CPI weights and net import reliance.

items.title: U.S. Equity Strategy: Second-Half Outlook
items.summary: Our base-case index earnings estimate is now $350 for ’26 and $400 for ’27, vs. our $320 estimate at the start of 2026. But we haven't increased our bull-case target of 8300. The implication is that we expect downside risks to increase relative to upside reward as the second half unfolds.

items.title: Global Asset Allocation: Extra Time for the AI Trade
items.summary: In the month since our last Global Asset Allocation, oil has collapsed to pre-war levels and the Fed has been surprisingly hawkish. This necessitates a change in our fixed-income allocation. We delve into the details and outline our strategic thoughts in this note.

items.title: PULSE Monitor: Broadening, or Tech Zero-Sum Game?
items.summary: Our latest sector recommendations focus on cyclical broadening as we move past peak geopolitical disruptions. Still, the AI portion of the S&P 500 must participate to deliver our $350 EPS estimate and 8100 price target. The issue now facing investors is whether Tech is a zero-sum game.

items.title: U.S. Airlines: Second-Quarter Preview
items.summary: Previewing airlines' second-quarter results, we see upside vs. consensus and even more upside ahead in the third quarter before our estimates normalize with respect to consensus. We're updating our Allegiant model given its merger with Sun Country, and upgrade ALGT to Buy/High Risk.

items.title: North America Insurance: Second-Quarter Preview
items.summary: The second quarter is likely to be a difficult growth quarter for insurers (bad news) due to property pricing pressure, but could end up being the hardest growth comp of the year (potential good news). We explain why we think estimate risk from changes to top lines should be less material.

items.title: Artificial Intelligence: The Open-Source Moment
items.summary: As regulators block access to some frontier models and slow the release of others, open models are gaining momentum as inference needs continue to outstrip supply, driving enterprises to seek out alternatives to frontier models for specific use cases. We explore this issue further herein.

items.title: China Internet: Valuation Floor Vs. Earnings Downside Sensitivity
items.summary: The significant underperformance of the China Internet sector has intensified, with the sector continuing to be a source of funds for the global AI hardware rally. This sell-off, however, has created a compelling valuation opportunity that we believe in, and hope is approaching a floor.

items.title: Global Economic Outlook & Strategy: Cascading Supply Shocks
items.summary: The U.S.-Iran interim deal has eased some key headwinds for the global economy, but it’s too soon to say the challenges are behind us. And a new risk to the global economy may be emerging in the form of a strong El Niño event that could bring substantial and long-lasting economic costs.

items.title: Sector & Industry Group Navigator: Back to Broadening
items.summary: U.S. equities have posted a strong second quarter with the S&P 500 up 13% as of this writing, and the AI trade a standout. We opportunistically reduce our Info Tech overweight while positioning for a cyclically led broadening call during the third quarter.

items.title: China's AI Supercycle Cuts Into the K-Shaped Economy
items.summary: AI is now front and center in China’s K-shaped economy, further powering exports, production and the new economy even as AI-driven job displacement weighs on consumer confidence and the significant AI buildout risks crowding out old-economy investment. We offer further analysis herein.

items.title: Through the Oil Shock: Five Lenses for ASEAN
items.summary: The oil shock that defined ASEAN this year is reversing as Brent falls back below $80 a barrel. We hold a Neutral weight on ASEAN given investors’ light positioning, except for Singapore. Within ASEAN, our preferred markets for 2026's second half are SG, MY, ID, PH and TH, in that order.

items.title: Slower Consumer Spending Keeps Rate Hikes Unlikely
items.summary: A large downward revision to first-quarter consumer spending more than offset any hawkish implications from expectedly strong May spending. Together with rising jobless claims, the data will challenge markets that have priced in a substantial probability of Fed rate hikes as early as July.

items.title: We're Upgrading L'Oréal to Buy
items.summary: We're upgrading L'Oréal to Buy and raising our target price to €435, anticipating a new, higher trading range for the stock later in the year.  We offer further details supporting our Buy thesis in this note and explain why we believe it is worth moving early.

items.title: Emerging Markets Strategy Weekly: A New USD Environment for EMFX
items.summary: We've been discussing the potential effects of rising U.S. real yields in global macro risk. The current macro backdrop still suggests resilience in the real rate valuation among high-yielding emerging markets, which could produce downside EUR-EM FX in some situations, as detailed herein.

items.title: Foreign Exchange Forecasts: June 2026
items.summary: We lower our 0-3-month EURUSD forecast to 1.13 to reflect USD-positive asymmetry around upcoming data and Fed pricing, as well potential momentum chasing on the bullish DXY break. In this scenario, EURUSD could overshoot our near-term forecast toward 1.10. We offer further analysis herein.

items.title: Micron Technology: It’s Different This Time
items.summary: We're buyers of Micron after the company signed Strategic Customer Agreements worth ~40% of sales to be covered over the next five years and talked about a transformed business model driven by memory shortages through 2028. We lift our target price for Buy-rated MU to $1,400.

items.title: European Rates Weekly: Summertime
items.summary: We expect the € front-end response to sharply lower oil prices to continue being relatively muted, with sticky hike premium also limiting the extent of belly richening.

items.title: What We See Ahead for Saudi National Bank
items.summary: Saudi National Bank is the best-performing name within our Saudi Arabia coverage. We sense momentum around some catalysts have started to slow and could remain muted. Nonetheless, valuations are still reasonable. We stay Buy-rated but position Riyad Bank and BSF as our preferred plays.

items.title: Poles' Rising Wealth Offers Financial-Services Opportunities
items.summary: We expect Poland's growing household wealth and increasing awareness of the need to save for retirement will lead not only to a further increase in the financial assets of Polish households but also to a shift in these assets' structure. We explore potential beneficiaries in this note.

items.title: We're Upgrading Four Chilean Banks
items.summary: We're upgrading our recommendation on Chilean banks as valuation ratios currently offer attractive discounts. We see two factors at work, as explored in this note. We're upgrading Santander Chile, Itau Chile and BICE to Buy from Neutral and BCI to Neutral from Sell.

items.title: Inside Our New Top Picks for Back Office Software
items.summary: We're updating our rankings across our back-office software coverage following first-quarter earnings, preferring names with a clear acceleration supported by differentiated AI strategies we see as underappreciated. Our new top picks are BOX, NAVN and APPN.

items.title: The Worst Is Likely Over for Systematic Curve Carry Strategies
items.summary: We expect commodities systematic curve carry strategies to continue to recover as oil prices trend lower over the coming six to 12 months. A major de-escalation is our new base case, and we view any temporary summer rally in oil prices as a rally to fade and a potential entry opportunity.
